1:
"And Miriam and Aaron spake against Moses because of the Ethiopian woman whom he had married: for he had married an Ethiopian woman." The very brief verse notes Miriam and Aaron speaking against Moses over his wife. The grammatical voices note the occasion. Henry and Gill note that "Miriam and Aaron spake against Moses because of the Ethiopian woman whom he had married" — Miriam and Aaron, Moses's own brother and sister, spoke against him, taking occasion from the Ethiopian (Cushite) woman he had married; the spake against Moses (the murmuring against God's chosen servant); the because of the Ethiopian woman whom he had married (the pretext, the marriage made the occasion); the figure (the sad sight of the nearest kin turning against God's servant, envy rising even in the family; the pretext of the marriage masking the deeper grievance of envy at Moses's place, vv.2; the murmuring against the man God had set over them; the warning that even the godly may fall into envy and detraction, speaking against the Lord's anointed, Psalm 105:15).

On "Miriam and Aaron spake against Moses," the witnesses note the sedition. Gill notes Moses's brother and sister speaking against him (the murmuring of the kin) — the sedition (the speaking against God's servant).

On "because of the Ethiopian woman whom he had married: for he had married an Ethiopian woman," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the pretext: "because of the Ethiopian woman whom he had married" — the marriage the occasion, the pretext for envy (Psalm 105:15); the figure (the deeper grievance masked); the providence (the murmuring, the claim of equal prophecy to follow, vv.2). The verse displays Miriam and Aaron speaking against Moses over his wife.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the occasion — "Miriam and Aaron spake against Moses because of the Ethiopian woman whom he had married," the sedition (Henry, Gill); "Miriam and Aaron spake against Moses," the sedition (Gill); "because of the Ethiopian woman whom he had married," the pretext (Psalm 105:15, Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the occasion. The verse marks the speakers: Miriam and Aaron. The verse displays Moses: spoken against. The verse displays the pretext: the marriage. The verse displays the envy: rising. The occasion displays the kin turning against God's servant. The verse displays the murmuring: of the family. The verse displays the pretext: the wife. The verse displays the envy: masked. Miriam and Aaron speaking against Moses over his wife displays the sedition of the kin: Miriam and Aaron spake against Moses because of the Ethiopian woman (Psalm 105:15) — the nearest kin turning against God's chosen servant, the marriage the pretext masking the deeper envy at Moses's place. The verse displays the claim of equal prophecy (v.2).
2:
"And they said, Hath the LORD indeed spoken only by Moses? hath he not spoken also by us? And the LORD heard it." The very brief verse notes the claim of equal prophecy and the LORD hearing it. The grammatical voices note the envy. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "they said, Hath the LORD indeed spoken only by Moses? hath he not spoken also by us? And the LORD heard it" — the real grievance: Miriam and Aaron envied Moses's unique authority, claiming they too were prophets; and the LORD heard their words; the Hath the LORD indeed spoken only by Moses? (the challenge to Moses's unique standing); the hath he not spoken also by us? (the claim of equal prophecy, Miriam a prophetess, Exodus 15:20, and Aaron the priest); the And the LORD heard it (God hearing the murmuring, the secret grievance not hidden from him); the figure (the envy of God's servant's special place, the grasping at equal honor; the pride that will not bear another's pre-eminence; the truth that God hears the murmuring of the heart, "the LORD heard it," nothing hidden from him, Malachi 3:16; the danger of envy at the gifts and place God gives to others).

On "Hath the LORD indeed spoken only by Moses? hath he not spoken also by us?" the witnesses note the claim. Gill notes the claim of equal prophecy (the envy of Moses's place, Exodus 15:20) — the envy (the grasping at honor).

On "And the LORD heard it," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note God's hearing: "And the LORD heard it" — God hearing the murmuring (nothing hidden, Malachi 3:16); the figure (the secret grievance known to God); the providence (the LORD hearing, the vindication of Moses to follow, vv.4-8). The verse displays the claim of equal prophecy and the LORD hearing it.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the envy — "Hath the LORD indeed spoken only by Moses? hath he not spoken also by us? And the LORD heard it," the envy (Keil, Gill); "Hath the LORD indeed spoken only by Moses? hath he not spoken also by us?" the claim (Exodus 15:20, Gill); "And the LORD heard it," God's hearing (Malachi 3:16, Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the envy. The verse marks the claim: equal prophecy. The verse displays Moses: envied. The verse displays the LORD: heard it. The verse displays the grievance: known. The envy displays the grasping at God's servant's place. The verse displays the claim: of equal honor. The verse displays the pride: rising. The verse displays God: hearing. The claim of equal prophecy and the LORD hearing it display the envy known to God: hath he not spoken also by us? And the LORD heard it (Exodus 15:20; Malachi 3:16) — the envy of Moses's unique place, the grasping at equal honor, and God hearing the murmuring of the heart, nothing hidden from him. The verse displays the meekness of Moses (v.3).
3:
"(Now the man Moses was very meek, above all the men which were upon the face of the earth.)" The witnesses note the meekness of Moses above all men — the crux of the meekness that figures Christ. On "the man Moses was very meek, above all the men which were upon the face of the earth," interpreters expound the testimony. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the meekness — "the man Moses was very meek, above all the men which were upon the face of the earth": in the midst of the attack on him, the Spirit records that Moses was the meekest of all men, that his vindication came not from himself but from God; the witnesses note the significance — the surpassing meekness of Moses (not defending himself, not resenting the wrong, leaving his cause to God); the lesson — that the meekness which does not avenge itself but leaves all to God is the highest grace (Moses, so provoked by his own kin, made no reply, but God arose to vindicate him; the meek who do not return the injury but commit themselves to the righteous Judge); the question of the authorship — some note that Moses, the writer, recording his own meekness, is no contradiction, for he wrote as moved by the Spirit, setting down the truth God testified, as he recorded his own faults elsewhere; the figure — Moses the meek a figure of Christ, "I am meek and lowly in heart," Matthew 11:29, "who, when he was reviled, reviled not again... but committed himself to him that judgeth righteously," 1 Peter 2:23; the meekness that inherits the earth, "blessed are the meek: for they shall inherit the earth," Matthew 5:5; the figure of the servant of God who endures wrong without retaliation, leaving his cause to God, supremely fulfilled in Christ the meek and lowly. The figure of meekness vindicated by God.

On "the man Moses was very meek," the grammatical-historical Keil and witnesses note the meekness. Keil and Ellicott note Moses very meek (not avenging himself, leaving his cause to God, 1 Peter 2:23) — the meekness (the highest grace).

On "above all the men which were upon the face of the earth," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the surpassing grace. Wesley and Gill note Moses the meekest of all (the surpassing meekness, the figure of Christ, Matthew 11:29) — the figure (the meek who inherit the earth, Matthew 5:5); the providence (the meekness recorded, God's vindication to follow). The verse displays the meekness of Moses above all men.

The witnesses agree the substance: Moses was the meekest of all men, leaving his cause to God (Calvin, Keil, Wesley, Ellicott, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of the meekness that figures Christ: the meekness of Moses — the surpassing grace that does not avenge itself but commits all to God (1 Peter 2:23), a figure of Christ the meek and lowly who, when reviled, reviled not again (Matthew 11:29; Matthew 5:5), the servant of God who endures wrong without retaliation and is vindicated by God. The verse displays the meekness crux. The verse marks Moses: very meek. The verse displays the men: above all. The verse displays the cause: left to God. The verse displays the Christ: the meek and lowly. The meekness displays the grace that does not avenge but leaves all to God. The verse displays Moses: silent under wrong. The verse displays the meek: vindicated. The verse displays the Christ: who reviled not again. The meekness of Moses above all men displays the crux: the man Moses was very meek, above all the men which were upon the face of the earth (Matthew 11:29; 1 Peter 2:23; Matthew 5:5) — the surpassing meekness that does not avenge itself but commits all to God, a figure of Christ the meek and lowly, the servant vindicated by God. The verse displays God calling the three to the tabernacle (v.4).
4:
"And the LORD spake suddenly unto Moses, and unto Aaron, and unto Miriam, Come out ye three unto the tabernacle of the congregation. And they three came out." The very brief verse notes the LORD suddenly calling the three to the tabernacle. The grammatical voices note the summons. Henry and Gill note that "the LORD spake suddenly unto Moses, and unto Aaron, and unto Miriam, Come out ye three unto the tabernacle of the congregation. And they three came out" — the LORD suddenly summoned all three to the tabernacle, to judge the matter himself; the the LORD spake suddenly (the swift summons, God acting at once for his servant); the Come out ye three unto the tabernacle (all three called to God's presence); the And they three came out (their obedience to the summons); the figure (God arising suddenly to vindicate his servant, the swiftness of his defense of the wronged; the summons before God who judges the matter; the truth that God himself takes up the cause of his servant, the wronged need not defend himself when God arises, Romans 12:19; the three brought before the LORD for judgment).

On "the LORD spake suddenly unto Moses, and unto Aaron, and unto Miriam," the witnesses note the summons. Gill notes the sudden summons of the three (God acting for his servant) — the summons (the swift call).

On "Come out ye three unto the tabernacle of the congregation. And they three came out," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the obedience: "Come out ye three... And they three came out" — all three called and coming (the summons obeyed, Romans 12:19); the figure (the cause taken up by God); the providence (the three before God, the vindication to follow, vv.5-8). The verse displays the LORD suddenly calling the three to the tabernacle.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the summons — "the LORD spake suddenly... Come out ye three... And they three came out," the summons (Henry, Gill); "the LORD spake suddenly unto Moses, and unto Aaron, and unto Miriam," the summons (Gill); "Come out ye three unto the tabernacle of the congregation. And they three came out," the obedience (Romans 12:19, Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the summons. The verse marks the LORD: suddenly. The verse displays the three: called. The verse displays the tabernacle: the place. The verse displays the cause: taken up. The summons displays God arising for his servant. The verse displays the call: swift. The verse displays the three: coming. The verse displays the judgment: of God. The LORD suddenly calling the three to the tabernacle displays the cause taken up by God: Come out ye three unto the tabernacle of the congregation (Romans 12:19) — God arising suddenly to vindicate his servant, the three summoned before him for judgment, the wronged needing not defend himself when God arises. The verse displays the LORD coming down in the cloud (v.5).
5:
"And the LORD came down in the pillar of the cloud, and stood in the door of the tabernacle, and called Aaron and Miriam: and they both came forth." The very brief verse notes the LORD coming down in the cloud to judge. The grammatical voices note the descent. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "the LORD came down in the pillar of the cloud, and stood in the door of the tabernacle, and called Aaron and Miriam: and they both came forth" — the LORD came down in the pillar of cloud, stood at the tabernacle door, and called Aaron and Miriam forth, apart from Moses; the the LORD came down in the pillar of the cloud (the visible token of God's presence descending to judge); the called Aaron and Miriam (the two offenders called apart, Moses not summoned to hear his own defense); the and they both came forth (the two standing before God); the figure (God descending to judge the matter, the presence in the cloud coming to vindicate his servant; the offenders called apart, that Moses not be present at his own vindication; the solemnity of God himself arising to defend the wronged and rebuke the offenders; the truth that God judges between his servants, the accusers brought before him, Psalm 50:21).

On "the LORD came down in the pillar of the cloud, and stood in the door of the tabernacle," the witnesses note the descent. Gill notes God descending in the cloud to judge (the presence coming) — the descent (God coming to judge).

On "and called Aaron and Miriam: and they both came forth," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the calling apart: "called Aaron and Miriam: and they both came forth" — the two offenders called apart (Moses not present at his vindication, Psalm 50:21); the figure (God arising to judge); the providence (the offenders before God, the rebuke to follow, vv.6-8). The verse displays the LORD coming down in the cloud to judge.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the descent — "the LORD came down in the pillar of the cloud... and called Aaron and Miriam: and they both came forth," the descent (Keil, Gill); "the LORD came down in the pillar of the cloud, and stood in the door of the tabernacle," the descent (Gill); "and called Aaron and Miriam: and they both came forth," the calling apart (Psalm 50:21, Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the descent. The verse marks the LORD: coming down. The verse displays the cloud: the presence. The verse displays Aaron and Miriam: called apart. The verse displays the judgment: of God. The descent displays God arising to judge the matter. The verse displays the LORD: descending. The verse displays the offenders: called. The verse displays the vindication: of Moses. The LORD coming down in the cloud to judge displays God arising for his servant: the LORD came down in the pillar of the cloud... and called Aaron and Miriam (Psalm 50:21) — God descending to judge the matter, the offenders called apart, the presence coming to vindicate his servant and rebuke the accusers. The verse displays God's testimony to Moses, the prophet unlike others (vv.6-8).
6:
"And he said, Hear now my words: If there be a prophet among you, I the LORD will make myself known unto him in a vision, and will speak unto him in a dream." The very brief verse notes God's ordinary way of speaking to prophets by vision and dream. The grammatical voices note the manner. Calvin and Gill note that "Hear now my words: If there be a prophet among you, I the LORD will make myself known unto him in a vision, and will speak unto him in a dream" — God declared his ordinary way of revealing himself to prophets: by vision and dream, not the close communion he had with Moses; the If there be a prophet among you (the ordinary prophet); the I the LORD will make myself known unto him in a vision, and will speak unto him in a dream (the manner of revelation to the prophets, indirect, by vision and dream); the figure (the lesser, mediated revelation given to the prophets, distinguished from the direct speech with Moses; the gradation of revelation, the prophets receiving by vision and dream, "God... spake in time past unto the fathers by the prophets," Hebrews 1:1; the contrast that exalts Moses's closeness, vv.7-8; the manner of prophetic revelation, the foreshadowing of the fuller revelation to come).

On "If there be a prophet among you, I the LORD will make myself known unto him in a vision," the witnesses note the vision. Gill notes the ordinary prophet receiving by vision (the mediated revelation, Hebrews 1:1) — the manner (the vision).

On "and will speak unto him in a dream," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the dream: "and will speak unto him in a dream" — the prophets spoken to in dreams (the indirect revelation); the figure (the lesser revelation distinguished from Moses's, Hebrews 1:1); the providence (the prophets by vision and dream, Moses's closeness to follow, vv.7-8). The verse displays God's ordinary way of speaking to prophets.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the manner — "If there be a prophet among you, I the LORD will make myself known unto him in a vision, and will speak unto him in a dream," the manner (Calvin, Gill); "If there be a prophet among you, I the LORD will make myself known unto him in a vision," the vision (Hebrews 1:1, Gill); "and will speak unto him in a dream," the dream (Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the manner. The verse marks the prophet: ordinary. The verse displays the vision: the manner. The verse displays the dream: the means. The verse displays the revelation: mediated. The manner displays the lesser revelation given to the prophets. The verse displays the prophet: by vision. The verse displays the dream: the means. The verse displays the closeness: of Moses contrasted. God's ordinary way of speaking to prophets displays the mediated revelation: I the LORD will make myself known unto him in a vision, and will speak unto him in a dream (Hebrews 1:1) — the lesser, mediated revelation given to the prophets by vision and dream, distinguished from the direct communion God had with Moses. The verse displays Moses faithful in all God's house (v.7).
7:
"My servant Moses is not so, who is faithful in all mine house." The very brief verse notes Moses faithful in all God's house. The grammatical voices note the faithfulness. Clarke and Gill note that "my servant Moses is not so, who is faithful in all mine house" — Moses was not like the ordinary prophets, for he was faithful in all God's house, trusted with the whole; the My servant Moses is not so (Moses above the ordinary prophets); the who is faithful in all mine house (the trusted steward, faithful over the whole household of God); the figure (Moses the faithful servant over all God's house, the steward trusted with the whole; the New Testament use, where Moses's faithfulness is the figure that exalts Christ: "Moses verily was faithful in all his house, as a servant... but Christ as a son over his own house," Hebrews 3:2-6; Moses faithful as a servant, Christ faithful as the Son and Lord of the house; the figure of the faithful steward, and of Christ the faithful Son over the house of God; the honor God puts on his faithful servant).

On "my servant Moses is not so," the witnesses note the distinction. Gill notes Moses above the ordinary prophets (not by vision and dream only) — the distinction (Moses unlike the rest).

On "who is faithful in all mine house," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the faithfulness: "who is faithful in all mine house" — Moses the faithful steward of the whole house (Hebrews 3:2-6); the figure (Moses faithful as a servant, Christ as the Son); the providence (the faithful servant, the mouth-to-mouth speech to follow, vv.8). The verse displays Moses faithful in all God's house.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the faithfulness — "my servant Moses is not so, who is faithful in all mine house," the faithfulness (Clarke, Gill); "my servant Moses is not so," the distinction (Gill); "who is faithful in all mine house," the faithfulness (Hebrews 3:2-6, Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the faithfulness. The verse marks Moses: the servant. The verse displays the house: all of it. The verse displays the faithfulness: trusted. The verse displays the Christ: the faithful Son. The faithfulness displays Moses the faithful steward of the whole house. The verse displays Moses: faithful. The verse displays the house: of God. The verse displays the Christ: over the house. Moses faithful in all God's house displays the faithful steward: my servant Moses... who is faithful in all mine house (Hebrews 3:2-6) — Moses the faithful servant over all God's house, a figure that exalts Christ the faithful Son over his own house. The verse displays God speaking to Moses mouth to mouth (v.8).
8:
"With him will I speak mouth to mouth, even apparently, and not in dark speeches; and the similitude of the LORD shall he behold: wherefore then were ye not afraid to speak against my servant Moses?" The witnesses note God speaking with Moses mouth to mouth, and the rebuke of the offenders — the crux of Moses's unique nearness to God. On "with him will I speak mouth to mouth, even apparently, and not in dark speeches; and the similitude of the LORD shall he behold," interpreters expound the vindication. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the close communion — "with him will I speak mouth to mouth, even apparently, and not in dark speeches; and the similitude of the LORD shall he behold": God spoke with Moses directly, mouth to mouth, clearly and not in riddles, and Moses beheld the form of the LORD; the witnesses note the significance — the unique nearness of Moses to God (the direct, clear, face-to-face communion, far above the vision and dream of the ordinary prophets, vv.6); the lesson — that God set Moses in a place of nearness above all, "the LORD spake unto Moses face to face, as a man speaketh unto his friend," Exodus 33:11; Deuteronomy 34:10, "there arose not a prophet since in Israel like unto Moses, whom the LORD knew face to face"; the gravity of the rebuke — "wherefore then were ye not afraid to speak against my servant Moses?" (the offenders rebuked for daring to murmur against the man God so honored); the figure — Moses's nearness a figure of the yet greater nearness of Christ, who is in the bosom of the Father and has declared him, "no man hath seen God at any time; the only begotten Son... he hath declared him," John 1:18; the warning against speaking against the Lord's servants, "touch not mine anointed," Psalm 105:15; the holy fear due to those God sets near himself; the figure of the danger of detraction against God's honored servants, and of the surpassing nearness of Christ to the Father.

On "with him will I speak mouth to mouth, even apparently, and not in dark speeches; and the similitude of the LORD shall he behold," the grammatical-historical Ellicott and witnesses note the communion. Ellicott and Gill note the direct, clear speech with Moses, beholding the form of the LORD (the unique nearness, Exodus 33:11; Deuteronomy 34:10) — the communion (the face-to-face nearness).

On "wherefore then were ye not afraid to speak against my servant Moses?" the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the rebuke. Wesley and Gill note the offenders rebuked for speaking against the honored servant (the holy fear due, Psalm 105:15) — the figure (the warning against detraction, John 1:18); the providence (the rebuke, the leprosy of Miriam to follow, vv.9-10). The verse displays God speaking with Moses mouth to mouth and rebuking the offenders.

The witnesses agree the substance: God spoke with Moses directly, mouth to mouth, and rebuked the offenders for speaking against him (Calvin, Ellicott, Wesley, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of Moses's unique nearness to God: the mouth-to-mouth speech — the direct, clear communion of Moses with God, far above the prophets' vision and dream, who beheld the form of the LORD (Exodus 33:11; Deuteronomy 34:10), and the grave rebuke of those who dared speak against the man God so honored (Psalm 105:15), a figure of the yet greater nearness of Christ in the bosom of the Father (John 1:18). The verse displays the nearness crux. The verse marks the speech: mouth to mouth. The verse displays the similitude: beheld. The verse displays the rebuke: of the offenders. The verse displays the Christ: in the bosom of the Father. The communion displays Moses's unique nearness to God. The verse displays the speech: direct. The verse displays the servant: honored. The verse displays the fear: due. God speaking with Moses mouth to mouth and rebuking the offenders displays the crux: with him will I speak mouth to mouth... wherefore then were ye not afraid to speak against my servant Moses? (Exodus 33:11; Deuteronomy 34:10; Psalm 105:15; John 1:18) — the unique nearness of Moses to God, the grave rebuke of those who murmur against his honored servant, a figure of the surpassing nearness of Christ to the Father. The verse displays the anger of the LORD and Miriam's leprosy (vv.9-10).
